text: The CNN Fear & Greed Index read 37 ('Fear') as of its last update on June 18 at 8:59 PM ET, unchanged from the prior close, with no June 19 update because US markets were closed for the Juneteenth federal holiday. The reading was down from 59 ('Greed') one month earlier, confirming that the relief rally had not repaired the deeper sentiment damage from the Fed-driven and Iran-war sell-offs.

European risk sentiment softened on June 19: the pan-European STOXX 600 closed down 0.2%, with mining (-2.1%) and travel and leisure (-0.9%) leading declines while energy (+1.3%) advanced on firmer oil, as investors reacted negatively to the postponement of US-Iran talks and fresh Israel-Hezbollah hostilities.

Asian sentiment was mixed: Japan's Nikkei 225 rose 0.28% to close at a fresh record of 71,250.06, even as the broader Topix fell 0.57% to 4,044.96, while South Korea's Kospi slipped 0.13% to 9,052.42 and Australia's S&P/ASX 200 declined 0.92% to 8,828.7, with markets in Shanghai, Hong Kong and Taiwan closed for the Dragon Boat Festival.

The VIX had no June 19 print because US equity markets were closed for Juneteenth and Cboe VIX derivatives settlement was delayed by the holiday; its last known level was 16.40 from the June 18 regular close, down 11.06% from the prior session, reflecting an unwound hedging bid but not a full risk-on regime shift.

UK 10-year gilt yields rose more than 7 basis points to 4.8247% after official figures showed government borrowing reached the highest level for May since 2019, with a budget deficit of £23.3 billion ($30.8 billion), adding a fiscal-risk overlay to the European sentiment backdrop.

US equity index futures pointed lower in thin holiday trading, with S&P 500 futures down 0.4%, Nasdaq 100 futures down 0.5%, and Dow futures down 0.3% as of 5:41 AM ET, suggesting the risk-on momentum from June 18 was fading into the weekend as investors re-assessed the durability of the US-Iran agreement.

Brent crude ticked higher on June 19, up 0.53% to about $80.38 a barrel by 1:30 PM ET, but remained set for a weekly fall of roughly 8%, as the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire and the slow restart of Hormuz traffic competed with Iran's new transit-conditions and delayed US-Iran talks.

Gold fell 1.59% to $4,143.20 per ounce on June 19, continuing its retreat from recent record highs above $5,100 as risk appetite held firm on the Iran deal and investors rotated away from safe-haven assets, though the precious metal remained up roughly 23% year-over-year.

subject.name: June 19 sentiment driver
subject.event: Fragile Iran deal execution, Hormuz transit conditions, Israel-Hezbollah flare-up and ceasefire

text: The dominant sentiment driver on June 19 was the visible fraying of the Iran-deal relief narrative: planned US-Iran talks in Switzerland were postponed, Israel and Hezbollah exchanged fire in Lebanon (16 killed; 4 Israeli soldiers dead), and Iran's Strait of Hormuz Authority issued an advisory requiring passage permits coordinated with the Revolutionary Guards navy, even as the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire took effect at 4 PM local time. Capital Economics' David Oxley warned 'the market might have gone a little bit too far,' LPL's Adam Turnquist saw 'pretty substantial risk,' RaboResearch's Bas van Geffen noted 'a strong undertow' beneath calmer waters, and Commodity Context's Rory Johnston observed 'that doesn't seem to be what we're getting' versus the seamless execution the market had priced.

The cross-asset risk map on June 19 was mixed-to-cautiously-risk-off: European equities dipped (STOXX 600 -0.2%, CAC 40 -0.55%, FTSE 100 -0.35%), Asian markets were split (Nikkei record high vs ASX -0.92% and Kosdaq -3.43%), US futures fell 0.3%-0.5%, gold sold off (-1.59%), oil eked out a small gain (+0.5%), and the CNN Fear & Greed Index held at 37 ('Fear'), painting a picture of a market pausing to re-price geopolitical risk rather than reversing the week's relief rally.

The Bank of Japan had raised its benchmark interest rate to a three-decade high of 1% earlier in the week, adding a hawkish counter-current to global rate sentiment: Japanese consumer prices excluding fresh food were unchanged in June data released Friday, though analysts expected inflation to pick up in coming months despite higher fuel costs, reinforcing the divergence between the BOJ's tightening and the Fed's hawkish-hold-with-hike-risk posture.

US equity and bond markets were closed on Friday June 19 for the Juneteenth federal holiday, with Cboe VIX derivatives settlement delayed and equity futures trading halted at 1:00 PM ET, meaning the authoritative US risk-sentiment prints for the date are the June 18 close carried through the weekend rather than fresh June 19 readings.
